quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "VSEPR theory"?

options.text: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion: electron groups around a central atom repel each other and arrange to maximize angular separation
The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu
A proton (H⁺) acceptor
ΔTb = iKbm; solute particles raise the boiling point by lowering the vapor pressure; i = van't Hoff factor

quiz_questions.explanation: "VSEPR theory" is defined as: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion: electron groups around a central atom repel each other and arrange to maximize angular separation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electron geometry"?

options.text: Properties that depend on the number of dissolved particles, not their chemical identity: ΔTb, ΔTf, π, ΔP
The exponent of a concentration term in the rate law; indicates how rate responds to concentration changes (0th, 1st, 2nd)
The amount of product actually obtained from a reaction; measured experimentally
The arrangement of ALL electron groups (bonding + lone pairs) around the central atom (e.g., tetrahedral for 4 groups)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electron geometry" is defined as: The arrangement of ALL electron groups (bonding + lone pairs) around the central atom (e.g., tetrahedral for 4 groups). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Molecular geometry"?

options.text: A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions
The arrangement of only the atoms (bonding pairs) around the central atom (e.g., bent for 2 bonding + 2 lone pairs)
The state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations are constant but molecular-level reactions continue

quiz_questions.explanation: "Molecular geometry" is defined as: The arrangement of only the atoms (bonding pairs) around the central atom (e.g., bent for 2 bonding + 2 lone pairs). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Hybridization"?

options.text: ΔTb = iKbm; solute particles raise the boiling point by lowering the vapor pressure; i = van't Hoff factor
Equilibrium involving species in more than one phase; pure solids and liquids have activity = 1 and are excluded from K
The mixing of atomic orbitals to form hybrid orbitals: sp (linear, 180°), sp² (trigonal planar, 120°), sp³ (tetrahedral, 109.5°)
If x/[initial] < 5%, the approximation [HA] ≈ [HA]₀ is valid; avoids solving the quadratic equation for weak acid/base equilibria

quiz_questions.explanation: "Hybridization" is defined as: The mixing of atomic orbitals to form hybrid orbitals: sp (linear, 180°), sp² (trigonal planar, 120°), sp³ (tetrahedral, 109.5°). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Dipole moment"?

options.text: An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product
Relates free energy to cell potential; n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)
A method for calculating equilibrium concentrations: Initial concentration, Change in concentration, Equilibrium concentration
A measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of all bond dipoles; symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄) can have polar bonds but no net dipole

quiz_questions.explanation: "Dipole moment" is defined as: A measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of all bond dipoles; symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄) can have polar bonds but no net dipole. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion: electron groups around a central atom repel each other and arrange to maximize angular separation"?

options.text: Colligative properties
Indicator
Polarizability
VSEPR theory

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "VSEPR theory".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The arrangement of ALL electron groups (bonding + lone pairs) around the central atom (e.g., tetrahedral for 4 groups)"?

options.text: Electron geometry
Formal charge
Rate law
Gibbs free energy (ΔG)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electron geometry".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The arrangement of only the atoms (bonding pairs) around the central atom (e.g., bent for 2 bonding + 2 lone pairs)"?

options.text: Stoichiometric conversion
Calorimetry
Common-ion effect
Molecular geometry

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Molecular geometry".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The mixing of atomic orbitals to form hybrid orbitals: sp (linear, 180°), sp² (trigonal planar, 120°), sp³ (tetrahedral, 109.5°)"?

options.text: Hybridization
Catalyst
Kw (autoionization constant)
Phase diagram

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Hybridization".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of all bond dipoles; symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄) can have polar bonds but no net dipole"?

options.text: ICE table
Viscosity
Green chemistry
Dipole moment

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Dipole moment".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Molecular Geometry is accurate?

options.text: Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.
Molecular Geometry is predicted by VSEPR theory (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion): electron groups around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion.
This allows calculation of ΔH for reactions that are difficult to measure directly, by combining known reactions.
Breaking bonds requires energy (endothermic), forming bonds releases energy (exothermic).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Molecular Geometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Molecular Geometry?

options.text: Entropy (S) measures the number of possible microstates (disorder).
Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) has a strong first dissociation and weak second (Ka2 = 0.012).
The excess reagent remains partially unreacted.
The number of bonding pairs and lone pairs determines geometry: 2 groups = linear (180°), 3 = trigonal planar (120°), 4 = tetrahedral (109.5°), 5 = trigonal bipyramidal, 6 = octahedral.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Molecular Geometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Molecular Geometry?

options.text: Oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions involve electron transfer; oxidation is loss of electrons (OIL), reduction is gain (RIG).
Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs, distorting bond angles.
Q (reaction quotient) compared to K predicts the direction a reaction will shift.
Equilibrium is dynamic (reactions continue but net change is zero).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Molecular Geometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Molecular Geometry?

options.text: Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.
The rate constant k varies with temperature.
Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity and geometry.
Kc uses molar concentrations, Kp uses partial pressures for gases (Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Molecular Geometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Molecular Geometry?

options.text: Molecular Geometry is predicted by VSEPR theory (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion): electron groups around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion.
K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Molecular Geometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Molecular Geometry?

options.text: Dissolution of ammonium nitrate: endothermic (ΔH > 0) but spontaneous because the large increase in entropy (ΔS >> 0) from ions dispersing in solution makes ΔG < 0
Aluminum production by Hall-Héroult process: electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite at ~960°C; consumes ~5% of US electricity production
Predicting reaction feasibility in industrial processes: ΔG calculations determine whether a proposed reaction path is thermodynamically viable before investing in equipment
Drug design depending on precise molecular geometry: the 3D shape of a drug molecule determines how it fits into an enzyme active site (lock-and-key model)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Molecular Geometry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Molecular Geometry?

options.text: Displacement reactions in metallurgy: extracting copper by placing iron in CuSO₄ solution (Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu, since Fe is more active than Cu)
Industrial removal of products: in esterification (alcohol + acid ⇌ ester + water), removing water with molecular sieves shifts equilibrium toward ester, increasing yield
Greenhouse gas behavior: CO₂ (linear, no permanent dipole) still absorbs IR radiation through asymmetric stretching, while symmetric stretching is IR-inactive
Radioactive decay following first-order kinetics with constant half-life: carbon-14 (t₁/₂ = 5,730 years) used for archaeological dating

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Molecular Geometry.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Molecular Geometry applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Carbon dioxide equilibrium in oceans (CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺) affected by rising atmospheric CO₂, causing ocean acidification
Ammonia's pyramidal geometry (sp³, one lone pair) making it a strong Lewis base and critical industrial chemical in fertilizer production (Haber process)
Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai): H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O generating electricity with only water as byproduct; zero emissions at point of use
Kidney stone formation: when [Ca²⁺][C₂O₄²⁻] exceeds Ksp of calcium oxalate (2.3 × 10⁻⁹), crystals precipitate in the kidney

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Molecular Geometry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Molecular Geometry?

options.text: Electric vehicle batteries: Tesla uses lithium-ion cells (LiNiCoAlO₂ cathode) delivering ~250 Wh/kg, enabling 300+ mile ranges
Quality control in manufacturing: monitoring percent yield to detect equipment problems, contaminated reagents, or process deviations
Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content
Drug design depending on precise molecular geometry: the 3D shape of a drug molecule determines how it fits into an enzyme active site (lock-and-key model)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Molecular Geometry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Molecular Geometry in practice?

options.text: Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data
Ammonia synthesis economics: K for N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is large at low T (favors products) but reaction is too slow; industrial compromise at ~450°C, 200 atm gives usable rate and yield
Greenhouse gas behavior: CO₂ (linear, no permanent dipole) still absorbs IR radiation through asymmetric stretching, while symmetric stretching is IR-inactive
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Molecular Geometry.
